# Transcrição: 03-3.0 - Scenes

- **Fonte:** 03-3.0 - Scenes
- **Duração:** 18:24
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

All right. So you have the frame for your story. You know where it begins and you know where it ends. Now it's time to fill in the middle. Remember in the beginning, I told you that storytelling was about two things, parts and decisions. This is where those parts come into play. We're going to be choosing the scenes for your story, the pieces that will create the puzzle that will ultimately be the story that you tell. Before we start talking about scenes though, we have to talk about chronology. How is your story going to play out in terms of a timeline? There's several choices. The most common way that a story is told is chronologically in order from beginning to end, meaning time will flow in one direction, from the past to the present. This is the most common way of telling a story because it's the way that most audiences expect stories to be told. We start at the beginning and we end at the end and we don't play with time in any way. And you should probably consider that chronology first for your story. This chronology works especially well with short stories, stories that take place over a short period of time, an hour, a day, sometimes even a week will be just fine with this chronology. I call it ABC, which means we're going to start in the beginning, we're going to move to the middle, and then we're going to end at the end. My story is probably going to be, almost certainly going to be, an ABC story, meaning I'm going to start with Charlie and the tube and I'm going to move through the story to the middle and then at the end I'm going to have my five second moment and I'm not going to need to play with time. I may pull in a little backstory. I may teach you a little bit about Clara and Charlie. I might provide some exposition, but I'm not going to move us backwards in time at any point. I'm going to keep the entire story flowing in one direction. That is the way more than half of the stories that I tell are told, but there are other chronologies to consider. The second most common chronology that I use is something I call B-A-B-C, and it's important to note that the second B is a lower case B. I'll explain why in a minute. B-A-C-B works really well if your story is long. If it's going to take place over the course of months or years, this is an ideal structure to use. It works well because most people don't want to hear long stories. They don't want to hear a story that's going to take place over years. What we do instead is we start somewhere in the middle. We start in the middle and it automatically shortens our story. Instead of being a story that begins way back in

## [03:00 - 06:01]

time, in the A part of the story and ending somewhere in the C, we start in the B in the middle. We move forward and eventually we're going to bring that past back in, but by starting in the middle it makes the story feel shorter. The best example of this is Forrest Gump. Forrest Gump is a movie about a man's entire life from his very earliest childhood experiences all the way to the point that he's, let's say, 60 years old and has a son going off to school of his own. No one really wants to hear a story that's going to begin in childhood and end in old age. What do they do in that movie? What does Robert Zemeckis do? He starts the story in the middle. Forrest is sitting on a bench. He's actually chronologically about five minutes from meeting his son. But in movie time, he's about an hour and 20 minutes before meeting his son. We start in the middle with Forrest on a bench with a box of chocolates. Someone sits down beside him. They ask him a question. He talks about his childhood. And then we come back to the bench. A new person sits next to him. He offers him some chocolates. He asks a question. Forrest talks about Vietnam. But we stay in the middle of the story. The story instead of Forrest as a child all the way to Forrest as an old man is Forrest in middle age moving ahead to Forrest in old age. A shorter story. And we use time travel. We manipulate time to bring in Forrest's earlier life. But by starting in the middle, it makes the story feel shorter. It doesn't burden us with the idea that we're beginning in childhood and we have to get all the way to old age, even though that's actually what will happen in the story. We also use this B-A-B-C structure if the opening scene of a story is horribly uncompelling. If the opening scene is just so unattractive, so unentertaining, that it's not going to launch our story in the way we want to. If you're burdened with that boring opening scene, you can move the story ahead and find a more interesting opening scene somewhere in the middle of the story and then eventually come back to the boring scene. And I can teach you ways to get through a boring scene and not allow it to be so boring. But by starting in the middle, we get to avoid a bad start. That lowercase b is important because it's a signal that we're going to start in the middle, and then we're going to go back in time for a while. And when we get back to that middle to that same spot, that second b, we're going to get through it quickly. That's why it's a lowercase b, because we should never repeat ourselves in a story. And in a B-A-B-C story, we're going to

## [06:01 - 09:03]

briefly repeat ourselves. We're going to bring force back to the bench with a box of chocolates, but we cannot linger there long or we will get bored. So the lowercase b is a signal to us that when we get back to the middle, we better usher through it quickly and then move on to the C. So we use the B-A-B-C, whether story takes place over a long period of time, or when our opening scene is not so good. Another option, which I use about 10% of the time, is the C-A-B-C, which means we're going to start at the end, and then we're going to work our way all the way back to the end. This only works if that final scene, that C, is so compelling, so mysterious, creates so much wonder in the audience's mind that they want to get back to it, that they want to know how did this happen to begin with? You need an ending that is just so strange that people desperately want to know, how did this happen? One of the stories that I use as a C-A-B-C story starts something like this. I'm standing in the broom closet in the basement of the Bourne police station in Bourne, Massachusetts. There's no light in this closet, so I'm standing in the pitch black. I've got one foot in a mop sink and the other foot on the floor because the closet is so small, it barely fits me. On the other side of the door, there are three police officers waiting for me to come out. They're waiting for me to confess to a crime I did not commit, and I think I'm going to do just that. I'm about to confess to a crime I didn't commit. That's the opening scene of my story. The next line says, I came to this police station three weeks ago, and you can see how I'm quickly going back to the A. I'm starting at the end, I'm in a broom closet in a police station in the basement with police officers on the other side of the door, and I'm about to confess to a crime I didn't commit. How did this happen? Let me tell you, three weeks before it all began. That is a C-A-B-C. I feel like the ending of that story is so strange and compelling that my audience will want to get me back there, that they want to know, how did you end up here? Let me explain to you. That's the purpose of the story. It only works when you have endings like this, an ending that demands explanation. How did this happen? Tell us the story and explain to us how you ended up in this position. I use that about 10% of the time. In other words, it's very unlikely that you're going to be using this chronological structure in your story, but it is possible. Those are the three most common chronological structures to stories, but there are others. The movie Memento is told backwards.

## [09:03 - 12:08]

I don't recommend it. I don't recommend it at all. It's been done once, well, I wouldn't try it, but it's up to you. Pulp Fiction is told out of order. The scenes are strategically mixed up. Again, I wouldn't recommend it, but it's your story. If you want to give it a try, go right ahead. My friend Monica was in a plane crash, and she told her story in a very unique way. Her story is not about surviving a plane crash because news flash, if the storyteller is telling you the story about the plane crash, you know she survived. Instead, Monica is a great storyteller. Her story is not about the plane crash at all. Monica at the time had a daughter who was dealing with anxiety. She needed to tell her anxious daughter that she was in a plane crash, but she didn't want her daughter so worried that she could never get into a plane again without her daughter sort of falling apart. So, Monica tells the story in what I call an A-B-A-B format, which is she tells the true story of the plane crash, and then she tells the sanitized version for her daughter. Then she tells us again a little more of the true horror of the plane crash, and then the much sanitized version that she tells her daughter. By going back and forth and back and forth, we get the true story and the fantasy. And throughout the entire story, the only thing we're worried about is not the plane crash or Monica's survival. We're thinking about a little girl, and is she going to hear the story, and is she going to be afraid and anxious the next time Monica gets in a plane? It's a brilliant way to tell a story. It will only work in very special circumstances like the one Monica happened to stumble upon. But the lesson here is if you have an unusual story, if you have a unique story, or you have a unique idea for chronology, give it a try. My recommendation though, at least for this first story, stick to the basics. A-B-C, if you can use it, it's going to be the best way to tell most stories. And if not, B-A-B-C, unless you have one of those remarkable and amazing endings, and in that case, C-A-B-C. You need the chronology in place before you start choosing how your scenes are going to play out, because your scenes are going to follow that chronology, and that is the next step. Once you've chosen your chronology, mark it off on your anatomy of a story worksheet. And just below that, you'll see some boxes where we're going to fill in the scenes. Stories are made up of parts, and the most significant parts of stories are scenes. Scenes are the physical locations in which your story will take place. You're going to identify those scenes in this next step. You're going to choose what happens where. Now, sometimes that

## [12:08 - 15:09]

can be tricky. My story is going to be tricky, because you might be thinking, in your story, Matt, you only have one scene. It's a McDonald's play place. Not true. My story has a multitude of scenes, just like yours will. Sometimes scenes take place in actual physical locations. I was standing in a store, and then I went home, and then I went to the backyard, and then I jumped into the pool. Those are naturally occurring scenes, and most stories operate in that way. But sometimes you end up with a story like mine. It takes place in one physical location, but still has many scenes. I'm going to break my scenes up in this way. The first scene is Charlie is up in the tube, and he gets stuck. Scene one. My second scene is Clara is sitting at a table eating pancakes. That's scene two. Scene three is going to be backstory. Let me teach you something about Clara while she's eating pancakes, and Charlie is stuck in the tube. That's scene three. Scene four. Let me teach you something about Charlie while he's up there crying in the tube. That's scene four. Scene five is me asking Clara to go climb up the tube and save her brother. Scene six is Clara eventually, reluctantly, climbing up the tower and crossing over to the cargo net to wait for Charlie. Scene seven. Two small hands crossing over a cargo net, coming together, and those beautiful words that Clara says. Seven scenes, one physical location, but lots of benefits from that. First of all, by breaking it into scenes, I will be able to move things around if necessary. If I decide to change the chronology of my story in any way, I now have blocks that I can move around. I can also drop blocks out. If my story ends up being eight minutes long, and I need it to be six minutes long, I might say to myself, I'm going to take away the stuff I'm going to teach about Charlie. It's not that important that you know about Charlie. It's more important that you know about Clara. So I'm going to take that scene right out and toss it aside. You don't need to know anything about Charlie except he's three or four years old and he's crying. That's good enough for the story if it needs to be shorter. It allows me to take things out, remove things, and even, in some cases, add a scene later on. If someone's wondering something, if I discover that my audience is curious about something that I've left out of the story, I can insert a new scene. It's also going to be extremely helpful when it comes time to memorize my story or what I like to say, remember my story. Because I'm not going to memorize word for word, but what I will memorize is scenes. I will remember the scenes, how they play out. I have to start my story with Charlie up in a tube stuck. And then I have to shift my gaze down to scene two, which is Clara at a table. And then I have to shift the audience a little bit into the past to teach them stuff about Clara, that's scene

## [15:09 - 18:10]

three. By remembering the scenes, I'll remember my story. I'll remember what I need to say, what I need to speak. So you're going to fill in your scenes that way. Now people do it in lots of different ways. I am not a visual learner. I'm an auditory learner. I remember what I hear, but not what I see. So for me, pictures aren't very helpful. But most people are actually visual learners. So many of the people I work with in these scene boxes, they actually draw out the scene. They'll draw stick figures or even more detailed drawings in each one of these boxes. Some people will actually grab comic book paper, which you can find on the internet, many different versions of comic book paper. I have storytellers that will find comic book paper where the size of the panes match the size of the scene. So small scenes get small comic book pains and larger scenes get larger comic book pains. Some people like to write in the pertinent facts that need to be included in each one of these scenes or a line they never want to lose, a great joke that they want to land inside that scene. Sometimes people will create the transitions between the scenes. They'll actually write the transition sentence between the boxes. So they remember how to get from one scene to the other. But your job is to make sure that you're telling your story in scenes. And the most important thing to remember when it comes to telling scenes and a story is every single scene has to have a physical location. In other words, your audience at all times must be able to see you in their mind's eye where you are. At no point in the story should the audience lose track of where you are physically in the world. Because as long as they can see you in a space, their imagination remains activated and their mind's eye remains open. The movie that you are creating in their mind continues to play as long as they can see you in a physical space taking advantage of the power of location. So that is your next step. Determine the chronology of your story and then decide what scenes will be included within your story, within the frame that we have created. Make sure that each one of those boxes represents a physical location. And if you're like me and everything takes place in one room, break that room, that space down into scenes that represent the different shifts that are taking place. The shift could be, I look up and I see Charlie, I look down and I see Clara, I go into the past and think about Clara, I go into the past and think about Charlie. So maybe your story takes place in a single chair and you never move, but your scenes are, I'm thinking of one thing and then I'm thinking of another thing. And then I hear something and then I see something and then I think something. All of those can be scenes too. You want to break your story down into parts, into physical locations, where are you and what action is taking place in that

## [18:10 - 18:24]

moment. And then fill in the box with any information that you think is going to be important. Go ahead and do those things now. And when you've picked your scenes and you've picked your chronology, go on to the next video. Good luck.
